# intro-demo

**End-to-end Chinese intro video via the full Reelforge pipeline.** Narration generated by a GPU TTS service (VoxCPM2 on a remote 5090), sentence-aligned SRT computed locally, rendered via the Chrome engine with TikTok-style per-word caption highlights.

## Output

- **47.64 s**, 1280 × 720 @ 30 fps, H.264 + AAC
- 8 SVG slides, one per sentence
- DOM-rendered TikTok captions (no libass dependency)

## How to rebuild

```bash
# 1. Synthesize narration on the GPU (VoxCPM2 on LAN host at 9094)
bun scripts/gpu-tts-to-srt.ts \
  --script examples/intro-demo/script.txt \
  --out examples/intro-demo/narration \
  --endpoint http://192.168.31.47:9094/v1/tts

# → narration.wav (48 kHz raw), narration.mp3 (re-encoded), narration.srt
#   (sentence-level timings — audio duration distributed proportionally to
#   each sentence's character count)

# 2. Build the 8 SVG slides (zero binary assets)
bash examples/intro-demo/build-slides.sh

# 3. Render the whole thing — byo-full mode (no ElevenLabs)
bun packages/cli/src/bin.ts generate examples/intro-demo/config.json \
  -o out/reelforge-intro.mp4 \
  --tiktok-captions
```

## Why SVG slides?

Homebrew's `ffmpeg` ships without `libass` / `libfreetype` → `drawtext` and `subtitles` filters fail. SVG ships the text as XML; Chrome (which already has its own font stack) renders it directly. Zero extra system dependencies.

## SRT timing strategy

The GPU TTS servers don't return word-level timestamps — they just hand back the rendered audio + a total duration. We split the narration on `。！？.!?` and distribute the audio duration proportionally to each sentence's **character count**:

```
sentence_duration = total_duration × (sentence_chars / total_chars)
```

This is enough precision to cut slides at sentence boundaries. For per-word caption highlighting you'd want a real STT pass (`reelforge stt narration.mp3 -o narration.srt` — one cue per word).

## Pipeline confirms two claims from DESIGN.md

1. **Reelforge consumes external TTS products** — no API keys, no provider lock-in. Any `audio + timings` pair works.
2. **DOM captions beat the ffmpeg subtitles filter everywhere that matters** — the final video carries WAAPI-animated TikTok captions baked into the pixels, and plays on any ffmpeg build.

See also the standalone utilities:

- `reelforge stt <audio> -o out.srt` — word-level transcription via whisper.cpp
- `reelforge captions narration.srt --to info` — inspect / validate / convert caption files
